narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your undergrad studies and LSAT prep, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped—landing at a 49% chance of not passing, which tipped the scales against admission. Picture it like this: the model looked at thousands of students like you and spotted patterns in who thrives in law school and beyond, and for you, your Year 3 decile of 9 stood out as the biggest signal pulling toward struggle, suggesting that even with solid performance, the intensifying coursework highlighted some hurdles others cleared more easily. Your Year 1 decile of 8 reinforced that early vibe, painting a picture of steady but not standout academic momentum in a competitive field. Layered on top was your lower-middle family income, which the model links to extra pressures that have tripped up others, making the long haul to the bar feel steeper. Your LSAT score of 19 whispered the same caution, as it often signals a tougher climb through the dense legal material ahead, while your undergrad GPA of 3.4, respectable as it is, didn't quite counterbalance that in the model's eyes. Even your Asian background nudged the odds downward in this context, based on past trends the model has learned. It's not that your full-time commitment or being a woman didn't help—they actually pulled in the positive direction, showing dedication and resilience that have carried many through—but they weren't enough to outweigh the heavier factors stacking up. This isn't about your worth or potential; it's the model connecting these dots from real stories of students who've walked similar paths, some soaring, others stumbling at the bar. The good news? You're already ahead with that 3.4 GPA and decile rankings—small shifts in study strategies or support could rewrite your story. Keep pushing; law schools need voices like yours. With encouragement, [Your Guide]
